  "order": [],
  "workable": [],
  "findings": [
    "Line and load conductors are reversed on the GFCI receptacle.",
    "Downstream receptacles lack ground-fault protection.",
    "Modern GFCI devices with line-load reversal will either fail to reset or will not provide power to the face, but older/defective units may still energize the face without providing actual ground-fault protection, creating a severe shock hazard."
  ],
  "actions": [
    "De-energize the circuit immediately at the main panel.",
    "Remove the GFCI receptacle from the outlet box.",
    "Identify the incoming hot/neutral supply wires (Line) and the downstream load wires (Load) using a voltage tester.",
    "Reconnect the incoming supply wires to the 'LINE' terminals and downstream wires to the 'LOAD' terminals according to manufacturer instructions.",
    "Reinstall the receptacle, restore power, and test the GFCI using the integral test/reset buttons and an external GFCI tester."
  ],
  "rationale": "A line/load reversal on a GFCI receptacle violates manufacturer installation instructions and NEC requirements. It prevents the device from functioning as intended, leaving downstream outlets unprotected and potentially leaving the GFCI face energized without active ground-fault protection, posing a critical shock and electrocution hazard.",
  "references": [
    "NEC 110.3(B)",
    "NEC 210.8"
  ]
}
